summary: Gruber collects Surface Pro reviews from major tech publications, all of which use the word 'compromise' to describe Microsoft's tablet-laptop hybrid — the exact opposite of Microsoft's relentless 'no compromises' marketing. He juxtaposes Steve Ballmer's and Steven Sinofsky's repeated insistence that Windows 8 devices would be 'no compromise' with reviewers unanimously finding the Surface Pro compromised as both a tablet and a laptop. Gruber also quotes his own 2011 piece arguing Apple had embraced compromise with iOS, then corrects himself: all design is about compromise. The real difference is that Apple and Microsoft chose to compromise on different things, with Apple's deliberate constraints producing better results in their chosen domain.
key_points: Every major Surface Pro review independently used the word 'compromise' to describe the device, directly contradicting Microsoft's core marketing message
Microsoft's 'no compromises' pitch promised a device that could fully replace both tablet and laptop, but reviewers found it inferior to both
Apple's iOS deliberately embraced constraints — limited multitasking, simpler interface — and those compromises became features that enabled simplicity and battery life
All design is inherently about compromise; the question is not whether to compromise but what to compromise on
The Surface Pro's attempt to be everything resulted in a device that was too heavy, too power-hungry, and too awkward for either use case
money_quotes: I was wrong to argue that only Apple had embraced compromise. The truth is, all design is about compromise.
Where Apple and Microsoft have forked is with regard to what they're willing to compromise on.
The compromises in iOS are, for many people in many contexts, what makes the iPad better than a Mac.
The compromises enforce simplicity and obviousness in design, and at a technical level they lead to iOS's excellent battery life.
The marvel of the iPad is not that it can replace a Mac. It's that it opened the door to all sorts of things that a Mac was never all that good for.
Putting the two together results in a breed that's simply not as compelling as separate tablets and laptops.
key_insight: All design is about compromise — Microsoft's failure with the Surface Pro wasn't in compromising, but in refusing to acknowledge that trying to be everything means excelling at nothing.
